title: Two Sum (hash map)
code: public int[] TwoSum(int[] nums, int target)

frames.caption: Walk left to right. For each x, look up its complement target − x in the map before inserting x, so a match always pairs with a strictly earlier index.
frames.note: The brute force checks every pair in O(n²). The hashing insight: a value x can only pair with its complement target − x, and a hash map answers "have I seen that value?" in O(1) average. Trading O(n) extra memory for O(1) lookups collapses the whole problem into a single O(n) pass.

frames.caption: i=0: x=3, need = 6 − 3 = 3. 3 is not in the map yet.
frames.note: Look up the complement BEFORE inserting the current value. Checking first guarantees the two indices are distinct (a value can't pair with itself) and that any match is with a strictly earlier element. The map stores value → index, not just membership, because the answer needs the partner's position.

frames.caption: Record 3 → index 0 in the map, then advance i.
frames.note: Only after the lookup do we record value → index, so the current element becomes a candidate partner for every element that comes after it. This "check, then insert" ordering is the heart of the one-pass technique — it is why a single traversal suffices instead of two.

frames.caption: i=1: x=2, need = 6 − 2 = 4. 4 is not in the map yet.
frames.note: Look up the complement BEFORE inserting the current value. Checking first guarantees the two indices are distinct (a value can't pair with itself) and that any match is with a strictly earlier element. The map stores value → index, not just membership, because the answer needs the partner's position.

frames.caption: Record 2 → index 1 in the map, then advance i.
frames.note: Only after the lookup do we record value → index, so the current element becomes a candidate partner for every element that comes after it. This "check, then insert" ordering is the heart of the one-pass technique — it is why a single traversal suffices instead of two.

frames.caption: i=2: x=4, need = 6 − 4 = 2. 2 is already in the map at index 1 → return [1, 2].
frames.note: The complement was stored on an earlier iteration, so we already have both indices: the earlier one from the map and the current i. Contrast this with the sorted-array two-pointer Two Sum (LC 167) — that is O(1) space but needs sorted input; the hash-map version works on unsorted data at the cost of O(n) space.
